wiki_catalogs.name: Tool Overview & Core Value
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for this section focused on the aicommit CLI tool. Explain its purpose as an AI-powered Git commit message generator that leverages LLMs via multiple providers (OpenRouter, Ollama, OpenAI-compatible APIs). Describe the architecture as a Rust-based binary with Node.js wrappers for npm distribution and VS Code integration. Highlight key features including automated commit generation, version synchronization across Cargo.toml and package.json, watch mode for auto-committing, and model jail/blacklist system. Document how it integrates into developer workflows through CLI, git hooks, and IDE extensions. Include practical examples of common use cases such as regular commits, version releases, and interactive editing. Address the relationship between components: how the Rust core handles logic while JavaScript files enable broader ecosystem integration. Provide conceptual overviews suitable for beginners and technical depth for experienced developers regarding performance, reliability, and extensibility.

wiki_catalogs.name: Global Settings
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed documentation for the global settings in the ~/.aicommit.json configuration file. Focus on fields such as 'active_provider' (UUID reference to the currently selected provider), and 'retry_attempts' (controls retry behavior on API failure, default 3). Explain how these settings affect overall tool behavior, including fallback mechanisms and user feedback during retries. Provide annotated JSON examples showing different configurations (e.g., increasing retry attempts for unstable networks). Describe how values are validated using serde, loaded via the dirs crate for cross-platform compatibility, and edited interactively through the --config flag. Include integration context with CLI arguments—explain precedence rules where command-line options override configuration file values. Address common issues like invalid UUID references or excessive retry delays and provide troubleshooting tips.

wiki_catalogs.name: Standard Mode
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the standard commit message generation mode in aicommit. Explain how the tool automatically generates AI-powered commit messages from Git diffs without user interaction. Document the underlying logic in src/main.rs where the diff is extracted, formatted into a prompt, and sent to the configured LLM provider. Include examples of command usage such as `aicommit` with no flags, and describe integration with automatic staging and committing. Clarify how this mode interacts with the active provider configuration and handles response parsing. Address performance considerations like API latency and token usage, and common issues such as vague or overly verbose messages. Provide guidance on optimizing prompts and selecting appropriate models for best results.

wiki_catalogs.name: OpenRouter Setup
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for OpenRouter provider integration. Explain how to obtain an API key from OpenRouter, configure it in the ~/.aicommit.json file, and select specific models using their model ID format (e.g., mistralai/mistral-tiny). Document the automatic cost tracking feature that fetches token prices via OpenRouter's API. Include code examples from src/main.rs showing the ProviderConfig::OpenRouter variant initialization and serde deserialization logic. Describe the difference between standard model selection and Simple Free mode. Address common issues such as invalid API keys, rate limiting, and unreachable endpoints. Provide best practices for securing credentials using environment variables or configuration files with proper permissions.

wiki_catalogs.name: Installation
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the VS Code extension installation process. Explain two primary methods: installing from the Visual Studio Code Marketplace and building manually from source using the provided build.sh script. Document prerequisites such as Node.js and npm dependencies, and clarify how package.json defines the extension's metadata and scripts. Include step-by-step instructions for both approaches, highlighting common pitfalls like missing build tools or incorrect PATH configurations. Provide verification steps to confirm successful installation within VS Code. Address platform-specific considerations for macOS, Linux, and Windows environments. Link this process to the broader aicommit CLI tool requirements, emphasizing that the core binary must be installed and accessible in the system PATH for proper functionality.

wiki_catalogs.name: Model Jail System
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the Model Jail System, a self-healing mechanism that automatically disables underperforming models based on error rates tracked via the ModelStats struct in src/main.rs. Explain how failed API calls are monitored, scored, and used to trigger jail conditions. Document the in-memory tracking and persistence logic, including how jailed models are excluded from selection in Simple Free mode. Include configuration options such as failure thresholds and cooldown periods. Provide real-world examples of error scenarios (e.g., timeouts, invalid responses) that activate the system. Describe recovery workflows like manual unjail commands and automatic release after timeout. Illustrate integration points with provider selection logic and CLI commands (--unjail, --jail-status). Address performance benefits and reliability improvements enabled by this circuit-breaker-like pattern.

wiki_catalogs.name: Version File Incrementation
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for this sub-feature explaining how the version file incrementation works in aicommit. Document the `increment_version` function that parses and increments semantic version strings (e.g., '0.0.37' → '0.0.38') by modifying the patch number. Explain the algorithm's handling of edge cases like invalid formats or non-numeric components. Describe how the `update_version_file` async function reads, updates, and writes the new version atomically while preserving file encoding and formatting. Include error handling for file I/O operations and version parsing failures. Provide code examples showing typical usage patterns and integration with other version management features. Address common issues such as permission errors, concurrent access conflicts, and malformed version files.

wiki_catalogs.name: OpenRouter Provider Settings
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for OpenRouter-specific configuration within the ~/.aicommit.json file. Explain each field: 'id' (UUIDv4 identifier), 'provider': 'openrouter', 'api_key' (required for authentication), 'model' (must be a valid OpenRouter model ID such as 'mistralai/mistral-tiny'), 'max_tokens' (response length limit, default 200), and 'temperature' (creativity control, 0.0–1.0, default 0.3). Document how token costs are automatically retrieved from the OpenRouter API during request processing in src/main.rs. Highlight security practices including environment variable injection via OPENROUTER_API_KEY and secure storage. Provide real-world configuration examples and explain how model selection impacts cost and performance. Include debugging guidance for common issues like invalid API keys, unsupported models, and rate limiting responses.

wiki_catalogs.name: Installation Guide
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the installation process of aicommit. Cover all supported methods including npm installation via 'npm install -g aicommit', building from source using Cargo ('cargo build --release'), and direct binary downloads. Explain prerequisites such as having Node.js, Rust toolchain, or Python (for utility scripts) installed. Provide step-by-step instructions for each platform (macOS, Linux, Windows) with shell commands. Include post-installation verification steps like running 'aicommit --version'. Document environment setup requirements such as API keys for OpenRouter or local Ollama server configuration. Address common installation issues like permission errors, PATH configuration, and dependency conflicts. Reference the package.json bin field mapping to index.js and explain how npm links the executable. Make content accessible to beginners while providing troubleshooting guidance for advanced users encountering system-specific problems.

wiki_catalogs.name: Provider-specific Settings
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop comprehensive content for provider-specific configuration blocks within the ~/.aicommit.json file. Detail each field used in provider entries: 'id' (UUIDv4 identifier for the provider instance), 'provider' (discriminant string: 'openrouter', 'ollama', or 'openai-compatible'), 'api_key' (secure credential storage, optional for local providers), 'model' (selected model identifier), 'max_tokens' (response length limit, default 200), and 'temperature' (creativity control, 0.0–1.0, default 0.3). Explain how these settings are deserialized from JSON into the ProviderConfig enum in src/main.rs using serde. Highlight differences between providers—for example, OpenRouter automatically fetches token costs while Ollama allows manual cost tracking. Provide real-world configuration examples for each provider type. Discuss security considerations around API key storage and environment variable overrides. Include debugging guidance for misconfigured URLs or unsupported models.

wiki_catalogs.name: Interactive/Dry-run Mode
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the interactive and dry-run modes of aicommit. Explain how `--dry-run` and `--interactive` flags allow users to preview, edit, and approve generated commit messages before applying them. Document the implementation in src/main.rs using dialoguer for CLI prompts and temporary message storage. Provide concrete usage examples such as `aicommit --dry-run` and `aicommit --interactive`, detailing workflow differences from standard mode. Describe integration with Git operations—how changes remain unstaged until user confirmation. Address edge cases like empty suggestions or manual message rejection. Include tips for effective review and customization of AI output, and performance implications due to additional user input cycles.

wiki_catalogs.name: Simple Free Mode
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the Simple Free mode feature that automatically selects free models from OpenRouter. Explain how the bin/get_free_models.py script fetches and caches available free models into openrouter_models/free_models.json. Document how aicommit rotates through these models when one fails or becomes unavailable. Include usage examples with --provider simple-free flag and explain fallback behavior. Show how model success/failure stats are tracked in memory and influence selection. Reference relevant code in src/main.rs that handles model rotation and failure detection. Address troubleshooting steps when no free models are available or all are temporarily jailed. Highlight limitations such as potential latency variability and lower model quality compared to paid alternatives.

wiki_catalogs.name: Usage
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for using the VS Code extension in daily development workflows. Explain how the extension integrates into VS Code's built-in Git interface, allowing users to generate AI-powered commit messages directly from the source control panel. Describe the user interaction flow: staging changes, clicking the 'Generate Commit Message' button, viewing AI suggestions, editing if needed, and committing. Highlight key features such as real-time feedback, error handling display, and compatibility with multi-root workspaces. Provide practical examples of usage patterns for standard commits, version bumps, and interactive editing. Document any available keyboard shortcuts or command palette entries. Emphasize the seamless experience compared to CLI usage, particularly for rapid iteration and visual diff review within the editor environment.

wiki_catalogs.name: Smart Diff Processing
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for Smart Diff Processing, which enhances commit message quality by filtering noise and highlighting semantically meaningful changes in code diffs. Explain how git diff output is parsed, sanitized, and condensed using regex and heuristic rules to remove irrelevant modifications (e.g., formatting, comments). Detail preprocessing steps that preserve context around changes while reducing token usage. Discuss integration with LLM prompts to maximize signal-to-noise ratio. Use code insights from src/main.rs to show how diff chunks are structured before being sent to providers. Cover performance considerations such as memory efficiency and streaming processing to handle large diffs. Provide examples comparing raw vs. processed diffs and their impact on generated messages. Address customization possibilities through configuration or future extensibility hooks.

wiki_catalogs.name: Cargo.toml Synchronization
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for this sub-feature explaining how aicommit synchronizes versions across Cargo.toml and Cargo.lock files. Document the internal logic in the `update_cargo_version` function that parses TOML content line-by-line to locate and update the version field without altering other configuration. Detail how the tool maintains consistency by also updating the version in Cargo.lock under the [package] section for 'aicommit'. Explain the post-update execution of 'cargo update --package aicommit' to ensure dependency resolution integrity. Cover error conditions such as missing manifest files, parse failures, or command execution errors. Provide practical examples demonstrating successful synchronization and recovery from common failure scenarios like merge conflicts during version bumps.

wiki_catalogs.name: Ollama Provider Settings
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop comprehensive content for Ollama-specific configuration in the ~/.aicommit.json file. Detail each field: 'id' (UUIDv4), 'provider': 'ollama', 'api_key' (optional, since Ollama typically runs locally without authentication), 'model' (local model name, e.g., 'llama3' or 'mistral'), 'max_tokens' (default 200), and 'temperature' (default 0.3). Explain how the tool communicates with the local Ollama server at http://localhost:11434 by default, and how users can override the endpoint via configuration or environment variables. Discuss manual cost tracking capabilities where users can optionally define cost per token for usage monitoring. Provide practical examples of configuring popular Ollama models. Address troubleshooting steps for connection failures, missing models, and model loading errors. Include guidance on pulling models using the Ollama CLI before use.

wiki_catalogs.name: Quick Start Tutorial
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create a hands-on tutorial that guides new users through their first experience with aicommit. Start with initializing a Git repository and making sample code changes. Walk through generating a commit message using the default configuration, explaining what happens behind the scenes: diff extraction, LLM processing via OpenRouter's free models, and message application. Include interactive mode usage with --dry-run flag, showing how to review and edit suggestions. Demonstrate adding a provider using --add-provider openrouter. Show basic version incrementation with --version-patch. Use real command examples from the readme.md and explain expected outputs. Emphasize the zero-configuration value proposition while hinting at advanced capabilities. Ensure the flow mirrors actual user onboarding journey and prepares them for deeper exploration of configuration and features.

wiki_catalogs.name: Advanced Configuration
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Document advanced configuration options that influence internal tool behavior beyond basic provider settings. Although not explicitly listed in the README, infer potential advanced parameters from the codebase such as diff formatting rules (e.g., context line limits), timeout thresholds for LLM requests, custom cost tracking for Ollama, and logging verbosity levels. Explain how these settings can be added manually to the configuration file and their impact on performance and reliability. Describe the underlying implementation using lazy_static and env_logger for runtime tuning, and how dialoguer enables interactive reconfiguration. Provide use cases like optimizing for low-latency connections or reducing token usage in constrained environments. Include guidance on validating schema compliance when extending the config manually and warn against invalid field names due to strict serde parsing.

wiki_catalogs.name: Watch Mode
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the watch mode functionality in aicommit, activated via `--watch`. Explain how the tool monitors file system changes using async event listeners and automatically generates and applies commits when modifications are detected. Document the implementation in src/main.rs using Tokio and filesystem watchers, including delay mechanisms (`--edit-delay`) to prevent premature commits. Cover use cases like continuous development workflows and automated environments. Detail interactions with Git operations, auto-staging, and optional auto-push features. Address performance considerations such as resource consumption during prolonged monitoring, network load from repeated API calls, and strategies to avoid rate limiting. Discuss troubleshooting common issues like missed events or excessive commits.

wiki_catalogs.name: Ollama Integration
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for Ollama provider integration. Explain how to set up a local Ollama server, pull supported models (e.g., llama3, mistral), and configure the endpoint URL in aicommit. Document how to specify custom model names and optional parameters like max_tokens and temperature in the configuration. Include code examples from src/main.rs showing the ProviderConfig::Ollama variant and its associated setup logic. Describe how users can optionally define custom token costs for usage tracking. Address common issues such as connection refused errors, missing models, and version compatibility between Ollama and aicommit. Provide debugging tips using curl commands to test Ollama health and model availability.

wiki_catalogs.name: Integration Details
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the integration architecture between the VS Code extension and the underlying aicommit CLI tool. Explain how extension.js communicates with bin/vscode-integration.js via child process execution and JSON-structured stdout messages. Document the message format used in the main() function of vscode-integration.js, including request/response schemas, error payloads, and status updates. Detail the data flow: from UI action in VS Code → IPC call → Node.js child_process spawn → CLI execution → JSON output parsing → UI update. Highlight how this design enables full feature parity with the CLI while maintaining responsiveness in the editor. Address debugging techniques such as logging JSON I/O, testing the integration script independently, and handling asynchronous operations. Discuss performance considerations like process startup overhead and strategies for minimizing latency during commit generation.

wiki_catalogs.name: Automatic Upstream Setup
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for Automatic Upstream Setup, a convenience feature that ensures proper Git tracking relationships when pushing commits. Explain how the system detects missing upstream branches and automatically configures them using 'git branch --set-upstream-to'. Document the underlying Git command execution via child_process spawning in src/main.rs. Describe detection logic that identifies untracked local branches and triggers setup only when necessary. Illustrate integration with push-after-commit workflows and watch mode operations. Highlight user experience benefits by eliminating manual upstream configuration. Include edge cases such as ambiguous remote names or permission issues. Provide troubleshooting guidance for common failures and explain how this automation fits into broader Git workflow optimization strategies within the tool.

wiki_catalogs.name: package.json Synchronization
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for this sub-feature explaining how aicommit updates version fields in package.json files. Document the implementation of the `update_npm_version` async function that uses serde_json to safely parse, modify, and serialize JSON content while preserving formatting and comments. Explain how the tool locates the top-level 'version' field and replaces its value with the incremented version string. Cover error handling for JSON parsing, file I/O operations, and schema validation. Discuss integration with npm scripts defined in package.json that may trigger or depend on version changes. Provide usage examples showing interaction between CLI flags like --version-npm and the resulting file modifications. Address common issues such as malformed JSON, permission restrictions, or concurrent write conflicts.

wiki_catalogs.name: OpenAI-Compatible Provider Settings
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed documentation for configuring OpenAI-compatible endpoints in ~/.aicommit.json. Cover all fields: 'id' (UUIDv4), 'provider': 'openai-compatible', 'api_key' (authentication token, if required by the endpoint), 'model' (model identifier accepted by the target service), 'max_tokens' (default 200), 'temperature' (default 0.3), and the requirement to specify a custom base URL via environment variable or wrapper configuration. Explain how this provider type enables integration with services like Azure OpenAI, Groq, or local inference servers (e.g., llama.cpp with OpenAI bindings). Describe the HTTP client setup in src/main.rs that dynamically constructs requests compatible with the OpenAI API format. Provide working examples for different backends. Include debugging tips for SSL errors, malformed responses, and authentication mismatches. Emphasize flexibility and interoperability considerations.

wiki_catalogs.name: Command Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for all CLI commands exposed by aicommit. Document each subcommand and flag parsed by clap in src/main.rs, including standard execution, --dry-run, --watch, --version-* flags, --add-provider, and configuration utilities. For each command, specify syntax, parameters, return values, and behavioral side effects (e.g., automatic staging, pushing). Provide concrete usage examples from actual code patterns in main.rs. Explain how commands interact with configuration state and Git operations. Address edge cases like empty diffs or failed API calls. Organize commands by functional group: commit generation modes, version management, configuration, and model control. Include performance considerations such as network latency in watch mode and token cost implications when using paid models.

wiki_catalogs.name: Version Management Commands
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for version management commands including `--version-bump`, `--version-file`, `--sync-cargo`, and related flags. Explain how these commands increment version numbers across package.json, Cargo.toml, and GitHub tags in a synchronized manner. Document the logic in src/main.rs that parses and updates semantic versions, writes to configuration files, and optionally creates Git tags. Provide real-world usage examples such as releasing a new patch version with `aicommit --version-patch`. Describe integration with Git operations and remote synchronization. Address edge cases like mismatched version formats or missing files. Include best practices for release automation and avoiding version drift between package managers.

wiki_catalogs.name: OpenAI-compatible Endpoints
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for integrating with any OpenAI-compatible API endpoint (e.g., local LLM servers, alternative cloud providers). Explain how to configure the custom base URL, API key, and model name in the provider settings. Document the expected API contract compliance (Chat Completions endpoint structure) and serialization format used in src/main.rs. Include examples of configuring popular services like LiteLLM, LocalAI, or Azure OpenAI. Show how the ProviderConfig::OpenAI variant handles authentication headers and request formatting. Address common compatibility issues such as non-standard response fields, streaming differences, and model name mismatches. Provide guidance on testing connectivity and validating responses using external tools before integration.

wiki_catalogs.name: Network Resilience
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for Network Resilience strategies implemented to ensure reliable communication with AI providers despite intermittent connectivity or service outages. Explain retry mechanisms with exponential backoff using tokio-based async delays and reqwest middleware. Document timeout configurations for connection, read, and write operations. Describe error classification logic that distinguishes transient failures (e.g., 5xx errors, timeouts) from permanent ones (e.g., 401, 404). Illustrate how these strategies integrate with provider request loops and model jail decisions. Use src/main.rs to highlight implementation specifics such as client builder settings and per-request timeout controls. Discuss trade-offs between aggressiveness of retries and user-perceived latency. Provide guidance on tuning network parameters for different environments (CI/CD vs. local development) and connectivity conditions.

wiki_catalogs.name: GitHub Release Creation
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for this sub-feature explaining how aicommit automates GitHub release creation through tag management. Document the `update_github_version` function that checks for existing tags using 'git tag -l', creates annotated tags with 'git tag -a v{version}' containing release messages, and pushes them to origin via 'git push origin v{version}'. Explain the semantic versioning convention used (v-prefixed tags) and how the tool prevents duplicate tag creation. Cover error conditions such as network failures, authentication issues, or repository state conflicts. Describe the integration workflow where version bumping automatically triggers release creation when --version-github flag is used. Provide practical examples of release automation in CI/CD pipelines and address troubleshooting steps for common git operation failures.

wiki_catalogs.name: Provider Configuration
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content covering all LLM provider integrations supported by aicommit. Detail setup procedures for OpenRouter (including API key management and model selection), Ollama (local endpoint configuration and model pulling), and generic OpenAI-compatible endpoints (custom URL and authentication). Explain the difference between Simple Free mode and custom model selection. Compare provider trade-offs in terms of cost, latency, privacy, and model quality. Document how provider configurations are stored in the ~/.aicommit.json file using serde serialization. Include code examples from src/main.rs showing how different ProviderConfig enum variants are initialized. Address common issues like unreachable servers, invalid credentials, and model compatibility. Provide best practices for securing API keys and rotating credentials.

wiki_catalogs.name: Configuration Commands
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for configuration-related CLI commands such as `--add-provider`, `--list-providers`, `--configure`, and `--reset-config`. Explain how these commands interact with the JSON configuration file (~/.aicommit.json), allowing users to set up providers, manage credentials, and modify global settings. Document the implementation in src/main.rs using serde for serialization and clap for argument parsing. Provide usage examples like `aicommit --add-provider openrouter <api_key>`. Describe how configurations are validated and stored securely. Address common issues such as malformed config files, duplicate providers, or permission errors. Include guidance on backup, migration, and environment-specific setups using environment variables.

wiki_catalogs.name: Provider Comparison
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content comparing all supported LLM providers (OpenRouter, Ollama, OpenAI-compatible) across key dimensions: cost (free vs. paid, token pricing), latency (cloud vs. local), privacy (data transmission risks), model quality, reliability, and ease of setup. Use real-world examples from the codebase showing different performance characteristics in commit message generation. Discuss trade-offs between using cloud-based services (higher quality, cost) versus local models (privacy, offline use). Include decision-making guidance for different user profiles (individual developers, teams, enterprise). Reference configuration patterns in src/main.rs that enable seamless switching between providers. Address hybrid strategies such as using Ollama by default with OpenRouter as fallback.

wiki_catalogs.name: Configuration File Reference
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed documentation for the ~/.aicommit.json configuration file structure used by the tool. Based on the Config struct in src/main.rs, document every field including global settings (default flags, dry_run behavior), provider-specific configurations (OpenRouter API keys, Ollama URLs), and advanced options (diff formatting rules, retry policies). Explain how configuration interacts with command-line arguments (precedence rules). Provide JSON schema examples with annotations for each property. Describe how the configuration is loaded, validated, and saved using serde and dialoguer. Include examples of common customization scenarios like changing default models or adjusting watch mode delays. Address data persistence mechanisms and cross-platform path handling using the dirs crate.

wiki_catalogs.name: Model Management Commands
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for model management commands including `--jail-model`, `--unjail-model`, `--blacklist-model`, and utilities for listing available models. Explain how the model jail and blacklist systems track failed models and prevent repeated usage attempts. Document the in-memory tracking and persistence mechanism in src/main.rs, referencing ModelStats and Config structures. Describe how `get_free_models.py` populates openrouter_models/free_models.json and its role in Simple Free mode. Provide examples such as `aicommit --jail-model claude-3-haiku` and `aicommit --list-models`. Address troubleshooting steps for stuck models, clearing state, and syncing with updated provider catalogs. Include performance benefits of avoiding failing models and reducing API error overhead.

wiki_catalogs.name: Version Management System
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Document the version synchronization capabilities of aicommit that ensure consistency across Cargo.toml, package.json, and GitHub tags. Explain how --version-patch, --version-minor, and --version-major flags trigger atomic updates to version fields in both manifest files. Detail the internal logic in src/main.rs that parses and modifies TOML and JSON files safely. Describe the optional GitHub release creation workflow, including tag naming conventions and push automation. Illustrate the complete release process from code freeze to production deployment. Discuss integration with CI/CD pipelines and semantic versioning best practices. Reference the package.json scripts that may interface with version operations and highlight error conditions like merge conflicts during version bumps.

wiki_catalogs.name: Advanced Features
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop in-depth content for sophisticated capabilities beyond basic commit generation. Cover the model jail system that automatically disables failing models based on error rates tracked in ModelStats struct. Explain smart diff processing techniques that filter noise and highlight meaningful changes. Detail automatic upstream branch setup that ensures proper tracking relationships. Describe network resilience strategies like retry logic and timeout handling implemented with reqwest and tokio. Discuss memory-efficient diff streaming and async operation coordination. Use code insights from src/main.rs to illustrate how these features improve reliability and user experience. Provide expert usage patterns such as combining watch mode with auto-push for unattended repositories.

wiki_catalogs.name: VS Code Extension
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive documentation for the VS Code extension included in the repository. Cover installation from the marketplace or manual build using build.sh. Explain usage within the editor's Git interface, including triggering commit generation and reviewing AI suggestions. Detail the integration architecture between extension.js and the backend script bin/vscode-integration.js that communicates via stdout JSON messages. Document the message format used in main() function of vscode-integration.js. Describe how the extension leverages the full power of the CLI tool while providing a seamless GUI experience. Address debugging techniques for extension issues and customization options available through VS Code settings. Highlight benefits over pure CLI usage for rapid iteration workflows.

wiki_catalogs.name: Development Guide
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Provide a complete guide for contributors and maintainers working on the aicommit codebase. Detail the build process using both Cargo and npm scripts, explaining how index.js wraps the compiled binary. Cover testing methodologies including unit tests, integration tests, and manual verification steps. Outline contribution workflows: setting up a development environment, running linters, submitting pull requests, and following code style guidelines. Explain the dual-language nature of the project with Rust as the primary implementation language and JavaScript for distribution and integration. Reference the dependencies listed in Cargo.toml and package.json, noting critical crates like clap, reqwest, and tokio. Include tips for debugging async operations and tracing HTTP requests to LLM providers.

wiki_catalogs.name: Troubleshooting
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create a practical troubleshooting guide addressing common issues encountered when using aicommit. Organize problems by category: authentication failures (invalid API keys, expired tokens), connectivity issues (timeout errors, DNS resolution), configuration problems (malformed JSON, incorrect paths), and operational errors (empty commits, failed pushes). For each issue, provide diagnostic steps using debug logs enabled via RUST_LOG, interpretation of error messages from src/main.rs, and verified solutions. Include guidance on checking model jail status and clearing blacklisted models. Address platform-specific quirks like Windows path handling or macOS security prompts. Recommend systematic approaches to isolate problems between network, configuration, and code states.

wiki_catalogs.name: API Reference (Internal)
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed API documentation for the internal Rust codebase of aicommit. Focus on public structs and enums exposed in src/main.rs including Config, Cli, ProviderConfig, ModelStats, and UsageInfo. For each type, document fields, methods, serialization attributes (serde), and their roles in the application lifecycle. Explain how the clap derive macros generate CLI argument parsers from the Cli struct. Document key functions like setup_openrouter_provider, execute_watch_mode, and handle_version_bump with parameter descriptions, return types, and error conditions. Illustrate data flow between configuration loading, provider initialization, and commit execution. Include examples of how components interact, such as how model failure statistics lead to jail decisions. This reference targets developers extending or maintaining the codebase.

wiki_overview.content:
<blog>
# Comprehensive Project Analysis of aicommit

## 1. Project Introduction

**Purpose Statement**:  
aicommit is a CLI tool designed to generate concise and descriptive Git commit messages using Large Language Models (LLMs). It automates the process of writing meaningful commit messages by analyzing code changes and leveraging AI to produce human-readable summaries.

**Core Goals and Objectives**:  
- Automate Git commit message generation using LLMs
- Support multiple AI provider backends (OpenRouter, Ollama, OpenAI-compatible APIs)
- Provide configuration flexibility for different development environments
- Enable version synchronization across Cargo.toml, package.json, and GitHub
- Facilitate integration with CI/CD pipelines and local development workflows

**Target Audience**:  
- Software developers seeking to improve Git commit hygiene
- Teams adopting AI-assisted development practices
- DevOps engineers managing automated deployment pipelines
- Open-source contributors looking to standardize commit messaging

---

## 2. Technical Architecture

### Component Breakdown

The project follows a modular Rust-based architecture with clear separation of concerns:

- **CLI Interface**: Handles user input and command routing (`src/cli/`)
- **Configuration System**: Manages provider settings and persistent state (`src/config/`)
- **Core Logic**: Orchestrates commit message generation workflow (`src/core/`)
- **Git Integration**: Interfaces with Git repositories (`src/git/`)
- **Provider Abstraction**: Supports multiple LLM providers via pluggable adapters (`src/providers/`)
- **Error Handling**: Centralized error management (`src/error/`)
- **Utilities**: Shared helper functions (`src/utils/`)

### Design Patterns

- **Command Pattern**: CLI commands implemented as discrete modules
- **Factory Pattern**: Provider creation abstracted through factory logic
- **Configuration-as-Code**: JSON-based config with schema validation
- **Async I/O**: Non-blocking operations using Tokio runtime
- **Modular Monolith**: Single binary with internal crate-like module structure

### System Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User] --> B[CLI Parser]
    B --> C[Command Router]
    C --> D[Workflow Manager]
    D --> E[Git Diff Analyzer]
    D --> F[Message Generator]
    F --> G[Active Provider]
    G --> H[OpenRouter|Ollama|OpenAI-Compatible]
    D --> I[Version Manager]
    D --> J[Git Commit]
    K[Config Loader] --> D
    L[Error Handlers] --> C
    M[UI Components] --> B
```

### Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Git Status] --> B[Diff Extraction]
    B --> C[Diff Truncation]
    C --> D[LLM Prompt Construction]
    D --> E[Provider API Call]
    E --> F[Response Parsing]
    F --> G[Commit Message]
    G --> H[Interactive Review]
    H --> I[Git Commit Execution]
```

---

## 3. Key Implementation

### Main Entry Points
- `src/main.rs`: Primary application entry point with argument parsing and execution flow
- `index.js`: Node.js wrapper for npm distribution
- `Cargo.toml`: Rust package manifest and build configuration
- `package.json`: NPM package metadata and scripts

### Core Modules
- `src/core/workflow.rs`: Coordinates the end-to-end commit message generation process
- `src/core/message_generator.rs`: Interfaces with LLM providers to generate messages
- `src/git/diff.rs`: Extracts and processes Git diff information
- `src/git/commit.rs`: Executes final git commit operation
- `src/config/loader.rs`: Loads and validates configuration from disk
- `src/providers/factory.rs`: Instantiates appropriate provider clients based on config

### Configuration Approach
- JSON configuration stored in `~/.aicommit.json`
- Environment variable support via dotenv
- Multi-provider support with active provider selection
- Schema validation in `src/config/validator.rs`
- Default `.gitignore` template management

### External Dependencies
- **Rust Crates**:
  - `tokio`: Async runtime
  - `reqwest`: HTTP client
  - `serde`: Serialization
  - `clap`: CLI argument parsing
  - `dialoguer`: Interactive prompts
  - `reqwest`: HTTP requests
- **Node.js Packages**: Available via `package.json` for npm distribution

### Integration Points
- **Git**: Direct shell invocation for status, diff, add, commit, push
- **OpenRouter API**: Through `src/providers/openrouter/client.rs`
- **Ollama**: Local LLM server integration
- **OpenAI-Compatible Endpoints**: For LM Studio and similar tools
- **VS Code Extension**: Located in `vscode-extension/` directory
- **NPM Registry**: Published as `@suenot/aicommit`

### Component Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    CLI[CLI Interface] --> Workflow[Workflow Manager]
    Config[Configuration] --> Workflow
    Git[Git Module] --> Workflow
    Workflow --> MessageGenerator[Message Generator]
    MessageGenerator --> ProviderFactory[Provider Factory]
    ProviderFactory --> OpenRouter[OpenRouter Provider]
    ProviderFactory --> Ollama[Ollama Provider]
    ProviderFactory --> OpenAICompatible[OpenAI-Compatible Provider]
    Workflow --> VersionManager[Version Manager]
    Workflow --> GitCommit[Git Commit]
    Error[Error Handlers] --> All[All Components]
    Utils[Utils] --> All
    UI[UI Components] --> CLI
```

---

## 4. Key Features

### Functionality Overview
- AI-generated commit messages from code diffs
- Multiple LLM provider support with fallback mechanisms
- Interactive mode for message review and editing
- Automatic version bumping across package managers
- Git hook integration capabilities
- Dry-run mode for testing
- Watch mode for auto-committing changes
- VS Code extension integration

### Implementation Highlights
- Smart diff truncation to stay within token limits (`MAX_DIFF_CHARS`, `MAX_FILE_DIFF_CHARS`)
- Preferred free model ranking system (`PREFERRED_FREE_MODELS`)
- Model jail/blacklist system for failed models
- Cross-platform compatibility (Darwin, Linux, Win32)
- Dual packaging (Cargo and npm)
- Built-in `.gitignore` management

### Feature Architecture

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> AnalyzingDiff: git diff
    AnalyzingDiff --> PreparingPrompt: truncate & format
    PreparingPrompt --> SendingToLLM: API call
    SendingToLLM --> ProcessingResponse: parse message
    ProcessingResponse --> ReviewMode: interactive edit
    ReviewMode --> FinalizingCommit: confirm
    FinalizingCommit --> ExecutingCommit: git commit
    ExecutingCommit --> VersionUpdate: --version-* flags
    VersionUpdate --> PushingChanges: --push
    PushingChanges --> Idle: complete
    ReviewMode --> Idle: abort
    ProcessingResponse --> ErrorHandler: API error
    ErrorHandler --> FallbackProvider: retry
    FallbackProvider --> SendingToLLM: alternate model
```
